params.text:
The four categories of abuse

Working Together recognises four broad categories of child abuse. A child may experience more than one at the same time, and abuse can be inflicted by an adult, by another child, or by a group.

  • Physical abuse – deliberately causing harm, for example hitting, shaking, throwing, burning, or fabricating the symptoms of an illness in a child.
  • Emotional abuse – persistent emotional maltreatment such as constant criticism, humiliation, threats, or making a child feel worthless or unloved. Some level of emotional abuse is present in all types of maltreatment.
  • Sexual abuse – forcing or enticing a child to take part in sexual activities, whether or not the child is aware of what is happening. This includes contact and non-contact acts, and viewing or producing sexual images.
  • Neglect – the ongoing failure to meet a child's basic physical or psychological needs, such as food, clothing, shelter, supervision, medical care, or emotional warmth.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Match each category of abuse to some of its common signs. Tap a card to reveal the indicators.

dialogs.answer: Unexplained bruises, burns or fractures; injuries in unusual places or of different ages; a story that keeps changing; a child who flinches at sudden movement or is reluctant to get changed.
Being overly anxious to please or withdrawn; extremes of passive or aggressive behaviour; delayed development; a fear of making mistakes; a lack of confidence or self-worth.
Sexual knowledge or behaviour that is not age-appropriate; soreness, bleeding or discomfort in the genital area; secrecy about an older 'friend'; sudden changes in mood, sleep or eating.
Being consistently dirty, hungry or inadequately clothed for the weather; poor hygiene; frequent illness that goes untreated; being left alone or unsupervised; missing medical or dental appointments.
No single sign proves abuse. Look for clusters, changes over time, and things that seem out of step with the child's usual behaviour or the explanation you are given.
